subject: Psychology
grade_subject_fit: Fits an introductory Psychology 11/12 lesson by establishing psychology as the scientific study of mind and behaviour, with emphasis on evidence, method, limits, and responsible explanation.
unit_context: Cycle A, Lesson 1. This lesson launches the foundations cycle by separating psychology from guessing, labelling, or pop-culture mind-reading claims.
assignment_purpose: Students learn to explain behaviour responsibly by moving from claim to evidence to method to limits.
final_evidence_target: A short exit response that defines psychology, separates mind from behaviour, and improves a weak explanation by adding evidence and limits.

student_task_flow.label: Part 1
student_task_flow.evidence: Students name mind and behaviour as the core focus.

student_task_flow.label: Part 2
student_task_flow.evidence: Students distinguish internal experiences from observable actions.

student_task_flow.label: Part 3
student_task_flow.evidence: Students replace a snap judgment with a cautious evidence-based explanation.

success_criteria: I can define psychology as the scientific study of mind and behaviour.
I can separate a mind example from a behaviour example.
I can explain why evidence is stronger than confident guessing.
I can include limits when I explain behaviour.
teacher_implementation_notes: Keep the classroom norm clear: psychology vocabulary is for responsible explanation, not labelling people.
Use the repeated chain claim, evidence, method, limits as the course-wide thinking move.
When students jump to one-cause explanations, ask what else could explain the same behaviour.
likely_misconceptions: Students may think psychology means reading people or guessing motives.
Students may treat one observed behaviour as enough evidence for a whole-person label.
Students may confuse a testable hypothesis with a broad theory.
Students may think confidence makes an explanation stronger than evidence.
assessment_focus: Clear distinction between mind and behaviour
Use of evidence and limits in explanation
Avoidance of overclaiming from one example

lesson_overview.overview: This opening lesson establishes psychology as a science of mind and behaviour. Students practise slowing down quick judgments and using evidence, method, and limits to build more responsible explanations.
lesson_overview.essential_question: How can psychology help us explain behaviour without reducing people to labels?

sequence.focus: Clear the myth that psychology is mind-reading or instant interpretation.
Define mind, behaviour, evidence, hypothesis, theory, explanation, and limits.
Use an example to separate evidence from explanation and include alternatives.
Students define psychology and improve a weak explanation.

integrity_checks: Do not let students diagnose or label classmates.
Require evidence and limits in every explanation.

teacher_guide.big_idea: Psychology is useful when it makes explanations more careful, evidence-based, and humane. The first lesson should break the habit of snap judgment and introduce a repeatable explanation frame.
learning_goals: Students can define psychology as the scientific study of mind and behaviour.
Students can distinguish internal mental processes from observable behaviour.
Students can describe why evidence, method, and limits matter.
Students can improve a weak explanation without overclaiming.

timing.activity: Open with common myths about psychology and separate entertainment claims from scientific explanation.
Teach the definition: scientific study of mind and behaviour. Model mind versus behaviour with a simple presentation example.
Introduce claim, evidence, method, and limits. Students complete Parts A and B of the task sheet.
Use the presentation-avoidance example to compare weak and stronger explanations. Students complete Parts C and D.
Students complete the exit ticket and name what should stick for the next lesson.

teacher_notes: When students say psychology is reading people, redirect to observable evidence and testable claims.
Keep the word limits visible: what does the evidence not show yet?
Use respectful phrasing: possible explanations, not labels.
Avoid using real students as examples. Keep scenarios fictional or generic.
look_fors: Student can define mind and behaviour separately.
Student can name evidence before explanation.
Student can improve a weak explanation by adding alternatives and limits.
Student avoids labelling a person from one behaviour.

content.subtitle: The science of mind and behaviour — not mind-reading, guessing, or labelling people.

content.scenario: Psychology is not reading people. It does not let us label someone from one classroom example.
prompts: What do people sometimes think psychology means?
What would count as better evidence?

content.left: Mind example: a student thinks everyone will notice if they make a mistake.
content.right: Behaviour example: the student avoids eye contact or asks to present later.
prompts: Which side can we observe more directly?
Why do we still need caution before explaining why it happened?

content.model: Stronger explanations move in this order: claim, evidence, method, limits.
supports: Claim: What are you saying is happening?
Method: How was the information gathered?
Limits: What else could explain it?

bullets: Evidence: information gathered through observation, measurement, or research.
Theory: a broad explanation supported by many findings over time.
Limits: what the evidence cannot show yet.

slides.title: Many causes, not one simple label

content.scenario: A behaviour can have several influences at once: emotion, attention, memory, sleep, motivation, family, friends, school, culture, past learning, habits, and recent events.
prompts: Why is one simple label usually too weak?
What would you need to know before explaining?

content.left: Weak: They avoid presentations because they are lazy.
content.right: Stronger: They may be avoiding because of worry, low preparation, past embarrassment, or another factor. We need more evidence.

prompts: Psychology is the scientific study of ____ and ____.
Name one mind example and one behaviour example.
Turn a weak label into a stronger explanation with evidence and limits.

rows: Psychology studies mind and behaviour scientifically.
Evidence is stronger than confident guessing.
A hypothesis is testable; a theory is broader and supported over time.
One behaviour does not equal a person’s whole identity.
Good explanations include context, possible causes, and limits.

task_sheet.title: What Is Psychology? — Student Task Sheet
task_sheet.purpose_line: Practise explaining behaviour with evidence, method, and limits instead of snap judgment.
instructions: Do not label a person from one behaviour.

tasks.label: Part A — Define the field
tasks.prompt: Write a clear definition of psychology using the words mind, behaviour, and science.

render_hints.heading: Define psychology
render_hints.help: Start with: Psychology is the scientific study of...

tasks.label: Part B — Separate mind and behaviour
tasks.prompt: For the presentation example, list one possible mind example and one observable behaviour example.

tasks.label: Part C — Evidence before explanation
tasks.prompt: What evidence would you need before making a stronger explanation about why someone avoided a presentation?

tasks.label: Part D — Improve a weak explanation
tasks.prompt: Rewrite this weak explanation: 'They avoid presentations because they are lazy.' Keep at least two possible explanations open and include a limit.

embedded_supports: Frame: The evidence we have is ___.
Frame: The evidence does not show ___ yet.
Reminder: a stronger explanation can keep more than one possibility open.
success_criteria: I included limits and avoided overclaiming.

exit_ticket.title: Exit Ticket — What Is Psychology?
exit_ticket.prompt: In 3-5 sentences, explain what psychology studies and why evidence and limits matter when explaining behaviour.
